major_gaps_text: - The final implication “no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\) for one fixed relative boundary piece \(X_B\) \(\Rightarrow M\) is properly proximal” is not justified and is not a consequence of the cited DKEP criterion. DKEP’s definition says absolute proper proximality is relative to \(K(L^2M)\), whereas the proof only proves a statement relative to \(X_B\). Since \(K(L^2M)\subset X_B\), one has \(S(M)=S_{K(L^2M)}(M)\subset S_{X_B}(M)\); absence of central states on the larger operator system does not rule out a central state on the smaller one, because central state extension is not automatic. DKEP’s monotonicity remark gives the opposite useful direction: absolute proper proximality implies relative proper proximality for larger boundary pieces, not conversely. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Routine patches for the relative statement:

1. **AFP patch.** Use the graph-product unscrewing theorem with
\[
\Gamma_1=\operatorname{Star}(v),\quad \Gamma_2=V\setminus\{v\},\quad \Gamma_1\cap\Gamma_2=\operatorname{Link}(v).
\]
Then the graph product is the trace-preserving amalgamated free product
\[
M=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}*_{M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Moreover,
\[
M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}\cong M_v\bar\otimes M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\]
and \(E_W\) kills reduced words containing a vertex outside \(W\).

2. **Patch for \(R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M)\).** For \(D\in\{A,C\}\) and \(x\in D^\circ=\ker E_B\), right multiplication by \(x\) preserves the AFP sectors whose first letter is \(D\), so \([R_D,\rho(x)]=0\). Since \(R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B\),
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=[e_B,\rho(x)].
\]
Because \(e_B\in X_B\) and \(JMJ\) acts by multipliers on \(X_B\), both \(e_B\rho(x)\) and \(\rho(x)e_B\) lie in \(X_B\subset K^{\infty,1}_{X_B}(M)\). The vertex algebras ultraweakly generate \(M\), so DKEP Lemma 6.1 gives \(R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M)\).

3. **Patch for \(c_1,c_2\).** If \(|N(v)|\ge2\), choose distinct non-neighbors \(w_1,w_2\); the words \(w_i\) and \(w_iw_j\) contain vertices outside \(L\), so \(E_B(c_i)=0\) and \(E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1\). If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), irreducibility gives \(z\in L\) nonadjacent to \(w\); then \(c_1=u_w\), \(c_2=u_z u_w\), and the reduced word \(wzw\) contains \(w\notin L\), so its \(E_B\)-expectation is zero.

4. **Patch for ping-pong.** In the AFP \(L^2\)-decomposition, left multiplication by \(d\in C^\circ\) sends \(L^2(B)\oplus\) first-\(A\) sectors into first-\(C\) sectors, hence \(dR_A d^*\le P_C\) for unitary \(d\in C^\circ\). Similarly, \(aR_Ca^*\le P_A\) for \(a\in A^\circ\). Since \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\), \((c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\perp H_A^+\), giving orthogonality of \(c_1R_Ac_1^*\) and \(c_2R_Ac_2^*\). The numerical contradiction then proves no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\).
problem_solved: Modulo the routine patches above, the proof establishes the following relative statement: for each vertex \(v\), with \(B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\), \(A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}\), and \(C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}\), one has \(M=A*_B C\), and the DKEP relative boundary operator system \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. Hence \(M\) is properly proximal relative to the boundary piece \(X_B\). The proof does not establish absolute proper proximality of \(M\).

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Graph-product/AFP patch.** Insert the following lemma before applying the decomposition:

   For every \(W\subset V\), \(M_W\subset M\) is the graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_s:s\in W\}\), and the trace-preserving expectation \(E_W:M\to M_W\) kills every reduced graph word containing a vertex outside \(W\). Moreover, for \(v\in V\),
   \[
   M \cong M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}*_{M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}M_{V\setminus\{v\}},
   \]
   state-preservingly, and
   \[
   M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}\cong M_v\bar\otimes M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
   \]
   These are exactly the standard Caspers–Fima graph-product results: their theorem gives the star/link AFP decomposition, their remark gives the conditional expectation behavior, and their star remark gives the tensor product identification. The hypotheses match because the present graph is finite and the vertex algebras are tracial von Neumann algebras. ([webusers.imj-prg.fr](https://webusers.imj-prg.fr/~pierre.fima/Documents/GraphProducts.pdf))

2. **Patch for \(R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M)\).** Let
   \[
   \mathcal I=K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M).
   \]
   Since \(X_B\) is the DKEP boundary piece generated by \(e_B\), we have \(e_B\in X_B\subset \mathcal I\). DKEP Proposition 3.6 gives that \(\mathcal I\) is a closed self-adjoint strong \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule. Hence for every \(x\in M\),
   \[
   e_B\rho(x),\ \rho(x)e_B\in \mathcal I,
   \qquad\text{so}\qquad
   [e_B,\rho(x)]\in\mathcal I.
   \]

   For \(D\in\{A,C\}\), the AFP normal-form calculation gives \([R_D,\rho(x)]=0\) for \(x\in D\), and therefore \([R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]\in\mathcal I\) for \(x\in D\). Thus, for \(T=R_A\), the commutator \([T,\rho(x)]\in\mathcal I\) for all \(x\in A\cup C\); similarly for \(T=R_C\).

   To extend this from \(A\cup C\) to all of \(M\), let \(\mathcal A\) be the algebraic \(*\)-algebra generated by \(A\cup C\). For products, use
   \[
   \rho(xy)=\rho(y)\rho(x),\qquad
   [T,\rho(xy)]=[T,\rho(y)]\rho(x)+\rho(y)[T,\rho(x)],
   \]
   and the \(JMJ\)-bimodule property of \(\mathcal I\). Hence \([T,\rho(z)]\in\mathcal I\) for all \(z\in\mathcal A\). Since \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\) as a von Neumann algebra, Kaplansky density gives uniformly bounded \(z_i\in\mathcal A\) with \(z_i\to z\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\). The estimate
   \[
   \|[T,\rho(z-z_i)]\|_{\infty,1}
   \le 2\|T\|\,\|z-z_i\|_2
   \]
   gives \([T,\rho(z_i)]\to [T,\rho(z)]\) in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\), so closedness of \(\mathcal I\) gives \([T,\rho(z)]\in\mathcal I\). Thus \(R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M)\), and hence \(P_A,P_C\in S_{X_B}(M)\).

3. **Patch for AFP sector inclusions.** In the AFP decomposition, for \(d\in D^\circ\), right multiplication by \(d\) either appends a \(D\)-letter or multiplies the last \(D\)-letter and decomposes the product into its \(B\)-part plus centered \(D\)-part. This never changes the first AFP letter, except that a one-letter \(D\)-word may collapse into \(L^2(B)\). Hence \(R_D L^2(M)\) and its orthogonal complement are invariant under \(\rho(d)\).

   Similarly, if \(d\in C^\circ\), left multiplication sends \(H_A^+=L^2(B)\oplus P_A L^2(M)\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\). Thus \(c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C\). If \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), then \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\), so for \(\xi,\eta\in H_A^+\),
   \[
   \langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
   =\langle \xi,(c_1^*c_2)\eta\rangle=0,
   \]
   because \((c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M)\perp H_A^+\). Hence
   \[
   c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
   \]
   The same argument with \(a\in A^\circ\) gives \(aR_Ca^*\le P_A\).

4. **Patch for the final relative conclusion.** By the DKEP definition, \(M\subset M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. The proof gives the stronger fact that no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\) exists at all. Therefore the relative conclusion follows. This does not imply absolute proper proximality relative to \(K(L^2M)\).

1. **Insert the precise DKEP facts.**  
   For \(B\subset M\), DKEP’s \(X_B\) is the boundary piece generated by \(e_BB(L^2M)e_B\) under left \(M\) and right \(JMJ\) multiplication. Thus \(e_B\in X_B\). Moreover
   \[
   \mathcal I:=K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M)
   \]
   is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed, self-adjoint, and is an \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule. Finally,
   \[
   S_{X_B}(M)=\{T\in B(L^2M):[T,JxJ]\in \mathcal I\ \forall x\in M\}.
   \]
   Since \(\rho(x)=Jx^*J\) ranges over \(JMJ\), the proof’s commutator condition with \(\rho(x)\) is equivalent to the DKEP definition.

2. **Justify that \(S_{X_B}(M)\) is an \(M\)-bimodule.**  
   If \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\) and \(m,n,x\in M\), then left multiplication by \(M\) commutes with \(JMJ\), so
   \[
   [mTn,JxJ]=m[T,JxJ]n\in \mathcal I.
   \]
   Hence \(mTn\in S_{X_B}(M)\). Therefore, for a unitary \(u\in M\), \(uTu^*\in S_{X_B}(M)\), and an \(M\)-central state \(\omega\) satisfies
   \[
   \omega(uTu^*)=\omega(T).
   \]

3. **Make the AFP sector lemma explicit.**  
   For \(M=A*_B C\), use the standard decomposition
   \[
   L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
   \bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
   K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
   \qquad K_D=L^2(D)\ominus L^2(B).
   \]
   Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\bar D\) be the other algebra. For \(d\in D^\circ=\ker E_B|_D\):
   - Right multiplication by \(d\) preserves \(R_D L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus P_D L^2(M)\) and also its orthogonal complement \(P_{\bar D}L^2(M)\). On a reduced tensor, if the last letter is not \(D\), one appends a \(D\)-letter; if the last letter is \(D\), decompose the product of the last \(D\)-letter with \(d\) into its \(B\)-part plus centered \(D\)-part. The first letter is unchanged, except that a one-letter \(D\)-word may collapse into \(L^2(B)\), still inside \(R_D L^2(M)\).
   - Left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_{\bar D}^+=L^2(B)\oplus P_{\bar D}L^2(M)\) into \(P_D L^2(M)\), because it either sends \(L^2(B)\) into \(K_D\) or prepends a centered \(D\)-letter to a word beginning with \(\bar D\).

   These assertions are first checked on algebraic reduced tensors and then extended by density and boundedness of left/right multiplication by \(d\). This supplies the rigor behind
   \[
   [R_D,\rho(x)]=0\quad(x\in D),\qquad
   c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C,\qquad
   aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
   \]

   Since the algebraic \(*\)-algebra generated by \(A\cup C\) is weakly dense in \(M\), Kaplansky density gives a uniformly bounded net \(z_i\) from that algebra with \(z_i\to z\) strongly, hence \(\|z_i-z\|_2\to0\). The displayed \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\) estimate then gives
   \[
   [T,\rho(z_i)]\to [T,\rho(z)]
   \quad\text{in }\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1},
   \]
   and \(\mathcal I\)-closedness yields \([T,\rho(z)]\in\mathcal I\).

   \[
   0\le \Omega(xq_X^\perp)\le \Omega(x),

   so \(\Phi|_M\) is normal by domination. The inclusion
   \(q_X^\perp\widetilde S_X(M)q_X^\perp\subset q_K^\perp\widetilde S(M)\)

4. **Finite-propagation lemma.** Replace the sketch by a formal normal-form induction. For fixed reduced Wick words \(a,b\), reduce \(ayb\) with \(y\in\mathcal A_W\). Only letters from the fixed words \(a,b\) can cancel letters of \(y\) outside \(U\); hence there are finitely many cancellation/shuffling diagrams. In each diagram, the uncancelled part of \(y\) that can survive \(E_U\) has vertices in \(U\cap W\), possibly further restricted to a subset \(J_r\subset U\cap W\) determined by commutation requirements with the fixed boundary letters. The fixed cancelled/moved letters contribute fixed algebraic multipliers \(\alpha_r,\beta_r,\gamma_r,\delta_r\), giving

   E_U(ayb)=\sum_r \alpha_r E_{J_r}(\gamma_r y\delta_r)\beta_r.
   \]

5. **AFP decomposition and ping-pong.** For fixed \(v\), set \(B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\), \(A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}\), \(C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}\). Since \(v\) commutes precisely with \(B\) inside \(A\), \(A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B\), and graph-product reduced words alternating between \(A^\circ\) and \(C^\circ\) are exactly the reduced words for \(A*_B C\). Thus \(M=A*_B C\). In the AFP \(L^2\)-decomposition, right multiplication by \(A\) preserves \(Q_A=e_B+P_A\) and \(P_C\), while right multiplication by \(C\) preserves \(Q_C=e_B+P_C\) and \(P_A\). Also, if \(c\in C^\circ\), then \(cQ_A L^2(M)\subset P_C L^2(M)\); if \(a\in A^\circ\), then \(aQ_C L^2(M)\subset P_A L^2(M)\). Orthogonality of \(c_1Q_A\) and \(c_2Q_A\) follows from \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\).

   For reduced algebraic words \(a,b\) and \(S,T\subset V\), there are finitely many algebraic words \(\alpha_r,\beta_r,\gamma_r,\delta_r\) and subsets \(J_r\subset S\cap T\) such that
   \[
   E_S(aE_T(x)b)=\sum_r \alpha_r\,E_{J_r}(\beta_r x\gamma_r)\,\delta_r
   \quad\text{for all algebraic }x.
   \]
   Proof: apply the Caspers–Fima normal-form reduction to the concatenation \(a\,E_T(x)\,b\). Only syllables of \(x\) that meet one of the finitely many syllables of \(a\) or \(b\) can be cancelled or absorbed. All other surviving syllables of \(x\) must already lie in \(T\), and survival after \(E_S\) forces them to lie in \(S\); hence their vertices lie in \(S\cap T\). This gives the displayed finite sum, hence

Let \(\mathcal K=K^{\infty,1}_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(X_L=X_B\) is the DKEP boundary piece for \(B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\), \(e_B\in X_L\), and \(M\) and \(\rho(M)=JMJ\) act as multipliers of \(X_L\) and hence of \(\mathcal K\). Thus for every \(x\in M\),
\[
e_B\rho(x),\ \rho(x)e_B\in \mathcal K,
\]
so \([e_B,\rho(x)]\in\mathcal K\). After verifying the commutator condition for \(R_A,R_C\) on generators \(A\cup C\), extend to algebraic words by induction using
\[
[T,\rho(xy)]=[T,\rho(y)\rho(x)]
=[T,\rho(y)]\rho(x)+\rho(y)[T,\rho(x)],
\]
and the stability of \(\mathcal K\) under left and right multiplication by \(\rho(M)\).

2. **Approximation and \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-estimate patch.**  
Let \(\mathcal A\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra. By Kaplansky density, for \(x\in M\) there are \(x_i\in\mathcal A\) with \(\sup_i\|x_i\|_\infty\le \|x\|_\infty\) and \(x_i\to x\) strongly; since \(\tau\) is finite, \(\|x_i-x\|_2\to0\). For \(y\in M\) and \(T\in B(L^2M)\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\|[T,\rho(y)]\|_{\infty,1}
&=\sup_{\|b\|_\infty\le1}
\|T\widehat{by}-\rho(y)T\hat b\|_1\\
&\le \|T\|\|y\|_2+\|y\|_2\|T\|
=2\|T\|\|y\|_2,
\end{aligned}
\]
using \(\|\widehat{by}\|_2\le\|y\|_2\), \(\|\zeta\|_1\le\|\zeta\|_2\), and \(\|\eta y\|_1\le\|\eta\|_2\|y\|_2\). Hence the commutator condition passes from algebraic approximants to arbitrary \(x\in M\).

3. **Hypertrace patch.**  
For \(N=zM\) with normalized trace \(\tau_z\), amenability of \(N\) is equivalent to existence of a hypertrace on \(B(L^2N)\). Equivalently, if \(N\) is injective, take a conditional expectation \(E:B(L^2N)\to N\) and set \(\psi=\tau_z\circ E\); then
\[
\psi(aT)=\tau_z(aE(T))=\tau_z(E(T)a)=\psi(Ta)
\]
for \(a\in N\), \(T\in B(L^2N)\). This is the needed \(N\)-central hypertrace. ([math.ucla.edu](https://www.math.ucla.edu/~popa/Books/IIun.pdf))
